title: Investigating Counterfactual Unfairness in LLMs towards Identities through Humor
venue: Proceedings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_questions: RQ1: Do LLMs refuse to generate humor differently depending on the speaker–target identities?
RQ2: Do LLMs attribute different speaker intentions depending on speaker–listener pairs, even when the humor content is identical?
RQ3: Do LLMs predict relational and societal impacts of humor asymmetrically across speaker–listener configurations?

datasets_used.name: Counterfactual humor fairness data
datasets_used.size: Task 1: 12,320 unique prompts per model and 61,600 requests and responses across five models; Tasks 2/3: 400 identity-agnostic jokes and 737 identity-specific disparagement jokes; Task 3 statistical dataset: 118,400 samples across five models

datasets_used.name: HaHackathon (SemEval-2021 Task 7)
datasets_used.size: 10,000 jokes from Reddit and Kaggle; each joke annotated by 20 raters for humor quality and offensiveness

methods_proposed.name: Counterfactual humor fairness framework
methods_proposed.summary: The paper probes identity-conditioned unfairness by swapping speaker, target, and listener identities while holding request templates or joke content constant across three humor tasks: generation refusal, intention inference, and relational/societal impact prediction.

methods_proposed.name: Asymmetric Refusal Rate (ARR)
methods_proposed.summary: ARR measures the absolute difference between refusal rates for reversed speaker–target configurations, |RR(A→B) − RR(B→A)|, to detect directional safety asymmetries.

methods_proposed.name: Speaker Effect (SE)
methods_proposed.summary: SE compares speaker-target conditioned refusal rates against target-only baselines, SE(A→B)=RR(A→B)−RR(B), to isolate whether a named speaker identity increases or decreases refusal.

methods_proposed.name: Difference-based Bias (B_diff)
methods_proposed.summary: B_diff averages valence-score differences between reversed speaker–listener configurations, mapping malicious to +1, uncertain to 0, and benign to −1 for speaker intention inference.

methods_proposed.name: Identity-specific disparagement humor curation pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: The paper combines GPT-4.1 pre-filtering with trained human verification to extract jokes with a single identity-based target and unanchored speaker–listener roles from HaHackathon.

humor_theories.detail: Identity-agnostic jokes are balanced across affiliative, aggressive, self-deprecating, and self-enhancing styles, and Task 2 asks models to classify speaker intention using the same framework.
McGraw and Warren (2010) is cited in the introduction to motivate humor’s dependence on social cues and acceptability boundaries.

evaluation_methods.detail: GPT-4o evaluates Task 1 refusal/fulfillment and Task 3 responses; Task 1 and Task 3 judge temperature is fixed at 0.
Llama-3-70B-Instruct is used as an independent cross-evaluator for the full Task 1 dataset and a 4,000-evaluation Task 3 subset.
Human experts validate Task 1 refusal labels, Task 3 score directions, and dataset filtering decisions.
The paper reports ARR, SE, B_diff, independent-samples t-tests, p-values, and Cohen’s d.
Two annotators filter identity-agnostic jokes; seven trained annotators verify identity-specific disparagement jokes, with each candidate reviewed by three annotators.

human_samples.observations: 10,000 HaHackathon jokes annotated for humor quality and offensiveness in the source corpus.
human_samples.detail: These are source-corpus annotations described by the paper, not newly recruited participants for this study.

human_samples.observations: Filtered 1,183 jokes from the Humor Recognition dataset to remove identity-related content, yielding 100 jokes per style and 400 total.
human_samples.detail: Used for the identity-agnostic humor subset.

human_samples.observations: Reviewed 1,224 candidate identity-specific samples; each sample was independently evaluated by three annotators; 737 jokes with unanimous agreement were retained.
human_samples.detail: Annotators manually verified filtering criteria, corrected mislabeled categories, and excluded intersectional and borderline cases.

human_samples.observations: Annotated 1,540 Task 1 samples for refusal/request fulfillment.
human_samples.detail: The subset was randomly sampled and balanced across models, identity configurations, and benign/malign request types.

human_samples.observations: Task 3 human validation is described as a subset of 800 samples; the same section also states 80 samples per model and 400 samples across five evaluated models, with two annotators assigned to each humor item.
human_samples.detail: Annotators scored model responses on humor acceptance, social sensitivity, and character consistency using the same 1–5 scale as the LLM judges.

judges.name: GPT-4o
judges.detail: Automated judge for Task 1 refusal classification and Task 3 humor acceptance, social sensitivity, and character consistency scores.

judges.name: Llama-3-70B-Instruct
judges.detail: Independent cross-evaluator for Task 1 and Task 3 validation.

judges.name: GPT-4.1
judges.detail: Used for automated pre-filtering and initial labeling of the identity-specific disparagement humor dataset.

judges.name: Claude 3.5 Haiku, GPT-4o, DeepSeek-Reasoner, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, Grok-4-Fast Non-Reasoning
judges.detail: In Task 2, the evaluated LLMs themselves output forced-choice humor style and intent-valence labels; these outputs constitute the measured labels.
One expert annotator validates Task 1 refusal classifications; three expert annotators validate Task 3 ratings.
Two annotators filter the identity-agnostic subset and seven trained annotators verify identity-specific disparagement-humor samples.

judges.name: ARR, SE, B_diff, t-tests, Cohen’s d
judges.detail: Metrics aggregate model outputs and judge labels into reported asymmetry and significance results.

generation_selection.detail: The paper reports 61,600 requests and responses across five models; no best-of-N selection is described.

generation_selection.n: one generated response per joke/identity/relationship variation
generation_selection.detail: Responses are later scored by GPT-4o; no candidate selection stage is described.
The task is classification with three trials and option shuffling, not open-ended generation.

model_provenance.name: Claude 3.5 Haiku
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated in all three main tasks and in the Task 2 intersectional appendix.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a target model; GPT-4o is also used as an automated judge, but the judge snapshot is not separately specified.

model_provenance.name: Grok-4-Fast Non-Reasoning
model_provenance.detail: Used in Tasks 2 and 3; tables also refer to Grok-4-Fast.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4.1
model_provenance.detail: Used for automated pre-filtering and initial labeling of the identity-specific humor dataset.

model_provenance.name: Llama-3-70B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Used as an independent open-source evaluator for Task 1 and Task 3 validation.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: temperature 0.7; system prompt: “You are a helpful assistant.”; no output-structure constraints
inference_budgets.detail: 80 templates instantiated into 9,680 speaker-target conditioned requests and 2,640 target-only requests, for 12,320 unique prompts per model and 61,600 requests/responses across five models.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: one GPT-4o evaluation per response; Llama-3-70B-Instruct re-evaluates the full 61,600 evaluations
inference_budgets.detail: Judge outputs structured JSON fields for contains_humor, humor_content, request_fulfilled, refusal_type, and deviation_type.
GPT-4.1 screens and labels HaHackathon jokes before human verification.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: three trials for each speaker–listener–joke combination
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Option order shuffled; “uncertain” varied with synonyms such as “unsure” and “undecided”; main-task temperature not reported
inference_budgets.detail: Models output exactly two words: humor style and intent valence.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: temperature 0.3 for GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Haiku
inference_budgets.detail: Uses 100 identity-agnostic humor items, 25 per humor style, across 24 speaker-listener pairs.
The statistical validation dataset comprises 118,400 samples across five models, 23,680 per model.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: one GPT-4o judge evaluation per generated response; Llama-3-70B-Instruct re-evaluates a 4,000-evaluation subset
inference_budgets.detail: Judge returns 1–5 scores and reasoning for humor acceptance, social sensitivity, and character consistency.

human_baselines.description: Human annotations are used for validation of LLM judges and dataset labels, but the paper does not report a human baseline system performing the same generation, refusal, or inference tasks under matched conditions.
human_baselines.detail: Task 3 humans score the same model responses, which validates evaluation direction but is not a baseline for model behavior.

mitigations: New request templates are constructed for Task 1.
Existing humor corpora are filtered and adapted for the counterfactual design.
Identity swaps hold joke/request content constant across conditions.
Human verification is used for the curated identity-specific subset.
contamination_risk.basis: The study uses newly constructed prompts and curated counterfactual conditions, but it also draws jokes from public pre-existing datasets such as HaHackathon and the Humor Recognition dataset; no decontamination test or model training-data audit is reported.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Task 1 refusal-rate and ARR claims and Task 3 humor-acceptance/social-sensitivity claims depend primarily on GPT-4o judgments; GPT-4.1 pre-filtering also affects the identity-specific dataset construction.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Task 1 human-vs-GPT-4o agreement is 96.88% (1,492/1,540), Cohen’s κ = 0.931; Task 3 human validation shows the same directional pattern for humor acceptance, social sensitivity, and character consistency, though character consistency agreement is weak.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The main conclusions are corroborated by Llama-3-70B-Instruct and human validation, but important headline metrics are still produced by LLM judges.

reporting_gaps: Inference dates are not reported for any model.
Access mode is not specified for the proprietary APIs or for Llama-3-70B-Instruct deployment.
The GPT-4o snapshot used specifically as judge is not separately specified from the evaluated GPT-4o model snapshot.
Main Task 2 temperature and other sampling parameters are not reported.
Task 3 target-model generation temperature and token limits are not reported.
Token budgets, compute, and monetary cost are not reported.
Human annotator recruitment, demographics, geography, and compensation are not reported.
The paper does not report a decontamination analysis for source jokes from public datasets.
Task 3 statistical validation notes that observations are not strictly independent because joke items appear across multiple speaker–listener conditions.
Character Consistency in Task 3 has weak human–LLM agreement, κ = 0.13–0.19.

headline_results.finding: In Task 1, the largest reported Asymmetric Refusal Rate is for the poor/wealthy pair, with Claude refusing one direction 67.5 percentage points more often than the reverse.
Averaging across Claude, GPT, DeepSeek, and Gemini, American→Chinese requests show 71% refusal versus 41% in the reverse direction.
For Task 2 identity-agnostic humor, able-bodied speakers addressing physically disabled listeners are labeled malicious at 46.2% versus 21.4% in the reverse direction.
In the unrelated-target Task 2 setting, White speakers talking to Black listeners about Chinese people are judged malicious 73.1% of the time versus 44.3% for the reverse configuration.
Across Task 2 models, unrelated-target identity jokes amplify average absolute B_diff by 1.71× to 3.96× relative to identity-agnostic humor.
In Task 3, overall humor acceptance is lower for Privileged→Marginalized than Marginalized→Privileged interactions, while social sensitivity is higher.

key_findings: Across models, humor requests from conventionally privileged speakers targeting marginalized groups are refused more often than the reverse, consistent with a learned punching-down versus punching-up hierarchy.
Models attribute more malicious and aggressive intent to identical jokes when the speaker has a privileged identity, even when the joke content itself is identity-agnostic.
Bias is amplified in Task 2 when jokes target an unrelated identity group, showing that speaker–listener identity cues can override the actual target of the joke.

Task 3 responses are judged as less accepting and more socially sensitive when privileged speakers address marginalized listeners, and professional hierarchies intensify these effects relative to friendships.

Intersectional complex profiles split models into conservative and lenient clusters: Claude, DeepSeek, and Grok treat complexity as a risk multiplier, while GPT-4o and Gemini treat it as added nuance.
The authors argue that models rely on demographic cues as proxies for harm rather than performing holistic context-sensitive social reasoning.
limitations: The study focuses on a small set of state-of-the-art proprietary models, excluding open-source systems and smaller checkpoints from the main evaluation.
The counterfactual design uses controlled identity swaps and curated templates, which abstract away nuance and spontaneity from natural conversations.
The datasets are limited to identity-agnostic jokes and disparagement humor derived from existing corpora, not the full diversity of humor.
The authors state that expanding to more varied humor sources and multilingual settings remains future work.
Task 3 statistical tests use samples that are not strictly independent because individual jokes appear across multiple speaker–listener conditions.
Task 3 Character Consistency scores should be interpreted cautiously because human–LLM agreement is weak for that dimension.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper includes an offensive-content warning. It studies disparagement humor and identity-targeted jokes, including potentially offensive examples. The Ethics Statement says AI assistants were used for language support, GPT-4.1 was used for dataset pre-filtering, GPT-4o for automated judging in Tasks 1 and 3, and automated outputs were validated through human annotation and independent cross-evaluation.

artifacts.other: The first page shows a GitHub icon followed by “Code and Dataset,” but the transcription does not provide a URL.
